pages.text:
THE USE OF AN INSULATOR COATING PRIMER
One of the important differences between CharCoat IC and other insulator coa(cid:415)ngs is that a separate CharCoat IC
Primer material is supplied for spraying on to the insulator prior to the applica(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng. This provides
important advantages as described below.
Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – this characteris(cid:415)c being the reason for its widespread
use in the manufacture of moulds, as the items are easily released once cured. This, however, is not a favourable
property for coa(cid:415)ngs and a bonding agent has to be used to ensure that the coa(cid:415)ng is well a(cid:425)ached to the insulator
and no peeling is experienced. Most coa(cid:415)ng manufacturers prefer to simplify the coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)on by mixing the
bonding agent into the coa(cid:415)ng itself. This has two main disadvantages, these being:
• Only a small percentage of the bonding agent ends up on the insulator surface – the remainder remains
within the coa(cid:415)ng layer and serves no useful purpose.
• Further, the presence of the bonding agent reduces the silicone content of the coa(cid:415)ng thus adversely affect-
ing its performance.
With the use of CharCoat IC Primer, not only is
the performance of the coa(cid:415)ng enhanced but
the adhesion to the insulator is superior.
This is well illustrated by subjec(cid:415)ng coa(cid:415)ng
test specimens to the 12 cycle, 100 hour
boiling test specified in Clause 6.6.1 of the
IEEE Std. 1523 for insulator coa(cid:415)ngs.
As shown in the adjacent image, the specimen
with the separate pre- applied primer
achieves far be(cid:425)er adhesion than when 10%
by weight of bonding agent is added to the
coa(cid:415)ng itself or when no primer or bonding
agent is used.
The full test is described in bonding test data
Having the bonding agent separate and used
as a primer, ensures that all of it is where it is
needed, i.e., at the interface between the
coa(cid:415)ng layer and the porcelain surface and,
moreover, the coa(cid:415)ng, being free of any extraneous material serving no beneficial purpose, has a higher silicone
content thus yielding excellent performance and a long life in extremely polluted environments.

rows: THE USE OF AN INSULATOR COATING PRIMER One of the important differences between CharCoat IC and other insulator coa(cid:415)ngs is that a separate CharCoat IC Primer material is supplied for spraying on to the insulator prior to the applica(cid:415)on of the coa(cid:415)ng. This provides important advantages as described below. Silicone rubber does not naturally adhere to other materials – this characteris(cid:415)c being the reason for its widespread use in the manufacture of moulds, as the items are easily released once cured. This, however, is not a favourable property for coa(cid:415)ngs and a bonding agent has to be used to ensure that the coa(cid:415)ng is well a(cid:425)ached to the insulator and no peeling is experienced. Most coa(cid:415)ng manufacturers prefer to simplify the coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)on by mixing the bonding agent into the coa(cid:415)ng itself. This has two main disadvantages, these being: • Only a small percentage of the bonding agent ends up on the insulator surface – the remainder remains within the coa(cid:415)ng layer and serves no useful purpose. • Further, the presence of the bonding agent reduces the silicone content of the coa(cid:415)ng thus adversely affect- ing its performance. With the use of CharCoat IC Primer, not only is the performance of the coa(cid:415)ng enhanced but the adhesion to the insulator is superior. This is well illustrated by subjec(cid:415)ng coa(cid:415)ng test specimens to the 12 cycle, 100 hour boiling test specified in Clause 6.6.1 of the IEEE Std. 1523 for insulator coa(cid:415)ngs. As shown in the adjacent image, the specimen with the separate pre- applied primer achieves far be(cid:425)er adhesion than when 10% by weight of bonding agent is added to the coa(cid:415)ng itself or when no primer or bonding agent is used. The full test is described in bonding test data Having the bonding agent separate and used as a primer, ensures that all of it is where it is needed, i.e., at the interface between the coa(cid:415)ng layer and the porcelain surface and, moreover, the coa(cid:415)ng, being free of any extraneous material serving no beneficial purpose, has a higher silicone content thus yielding excellent performance and a long life in extremely polluted environments.

Most coa(cid:415)ng manufacturers prefer to simplify the coa(cid:415)ng applica(cid:415)on by mixing the bonding agent into the coa(cid:415)ng itself.
